coredata.dc:title: Design and application of metal-organic framework based chemo-fluorescent sensor for selective sensing of the full spectrum of tetracyclines in complex samples
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Yaowei
Tang, Ruijie
Zhao, Jun
Jiang, Changlong
Li, Lingfei
Liu, Bianhua
coredata.dc:description: Tetracyclines antibiotics (TCs) are widely used as broad-spectrum antibiotics, but their overuse leads to environmental pollution and threatens human health. Existing detection techniques (mass spectrometry, etc.) are complicated and cumbersome, and do not allow for rapid detection. Consequently, it is imperative to develop a sensing technology for rapid on-site detection of full-spectrum TCs residues. The UV–visible absorption peaks of TCs overlap with the blue fluorescence excitation wavelength range, which can “turn-off” the blue fluorescence based on the internal filtering effect. Additionally, the β-diketone bond of TCs can bind to Eu3+, sensitizing Eu3+ to produce red fluorescence. Thus, we constructed a selective sensing chemo-fluorescent sensor using blue nitrogen-doped carbon dots (BCDs) and europium metal–organic framework (Eu-MOF). As the concentration of TCs increased from 0 to 2.6 μM, the sensor exhibited a continuous change in fluorescence color from blue to purple to red. The limit of detection is 1.37 nM with a response time of less than 1 s. Furthermore, this study presents a portable capillary chemo-fluorescent sensor detection kit, which consists of a capillary chemo-fluorescent sensor comprising BCDs/Eu-MOF encapsulated in a self-designed non-fluorescent glass capillary, as well as a color recognizer with a kit produced using 3D printing technology. This fluorescence sensing technology with excellent sensitivity and selectivity provides technical support for the rapid detection of TCs and the reduction of pollutant hazards to sustainable human development.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemo-fluorescent sensor
Fluorescent sensing technology
Tetracyclines full spectrum
Capillary chemo-fluorescent sensor
Ultra-fast
Ultra-sensitive

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of TCs by BCDs/Eu-MOF chemo-fluorescent sensor
Portable capillary chemo-fluorescent sensor detection kit for the detection of TCs
Detection of TCs in real complex samples
Design principle and mechanism study of the BCDs/Eu-MOF chemo-fluorescent sensor
Sensitive ratiometric fluorescence detection of TCs with BCDs/Eu-MOF chemo-fluorescent sensors
Selectivity and immunity of ratiometric BCDs/Eu-MOF fluorescent chemo-fluorescent sensors
Construction of a portable capillary chemo-fluorescent sensor detection kit
Detection of TCs in real complex samples

(KLAS2022KF001).

This work is financially supported by National Key Research and Development Program ( 2021YFD2000202 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos, 22274158 ), Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Provincial ( 2208085 \u202FMB30), Open project funding for the Key Laboratory of Agricultural Sensors of the Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, and Open project funding for the Key Laboratory of Smart Agricultural Technology and Equipment of Anhui Province. ( KLAS2022KF001 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD image of Eu-MOF, (b) XPS Eu 3d image of Eu-MOF, (c) Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms and pore size distribution of Eu-MOF (inset), (d) (e) SEM images of Eu-MOF, (f) TEM image of BCDs, (g) UV–vis absorption spectra of Eu-MOF, TCs and Eu-MOF + TCs, (h) FT-IR spectra of TCs, Eu-MOF and Eu-MOF + TCs, (i) Fluorescence spectra of BCDs/Eu-MOF at different excitation wavelengths.

Fluorescence spectra of BCDs/Eu-MOF sensors when different amounts of (a) DOC, (b) DMC, (c) MTC, (d) CTC, (e) TC, and (f) OTC were added, the inset shows the fluorescence color photographs under the irradiation of 350 nm UV lamp when the corresponding concentrations of TCs were added.
Linear fit plots of I616/I454 fluorescence intensity ratios of BCDs/Eu-MOF at different amounts of (a) DOC, (b) DMC, (c) MTC, (d) CTC, (e) TC, (f) OTC.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of BCDs/Eu-MOF sensors after the addition of 2.2 μM of various interfering substances, (b) Selectivity and anti-interference test of BCDs/Eu-MOF, (c) Fluorescence color photographs of BCDs/Eu-MOF after addition of different substances to BCDs/Eu-MOF using 350 nm UV lamp irradiation. Samples labeled 1–26 represent (1) Blank, (2) LCM, (3) PIPC, (4) CLI, (5) ROX, (6) CEZ, (7) VAN, (8) CAM, (9) PEF, (10) LOM, (11) GM, (12) AMX, (13) CEC, (14) Cr3+, (15) Fe3+, (16) Cu2+, (17) CYP, (18) GS, (19) TET, (20) DMC, (21) DOC, (22) MTC, (23) CTC, (24) TC (25) OTC, and (26) All, respectively.

(a) Parts of the portable capillary chemo-fluorescent sensor detection kit, (b) Schematic diagram of the RGB color recognition application for smartphone, (c) Linear fit plot of TC versus R/B at different concentrations.
Synthesis route of BCDs/Eu-MOF and its mechanism for detecting TCs (S denotes singlet states, T represents triplet states, ISC stands for intersystem crossing, and ET indicates energy transfer).
Determination of TC spiked in real complex samples by BCDs/Eu-MOF.

head.ce:title: Design and application of metal-organic framework based chemo-fluorescent sensor for selective sensing of the full spectrum of tetracyclines in complex samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026 China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Solid-State Physics, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, Anhui 230031, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Solid-State Physics, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, Anhui 230031, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tetracyclines antibiotics (TCs) are widely used as broad-spectrum antibiotics, but their overuse leads to environmental pollution and threatens human health. Existing detection techniques (mass spectrometry, etc.) are complicated and cumbersome, and do not allow for rapid detection. Consequently, it is imperative to develop a sensing technology for rapid on-site detection of full-spectrum TCs residues. The UV–visible absorption peaks of TCs overlap with the blue fluorescence excitation wavelength range, which can “turn-off” the blue fluorescence based on the internal filtering effect. Additionally, the β-diketone bond of TCs can bind to Eu3+, sensitizing Eu3+ to produce red fluorescence. Thus, we constructed a selective sensing chemo-fluorescent sensor using blue nitrogen-doped carbon dots (BCDs) and europium metal–organic framework (Eu-MOF). As the concentration of TCs increased from 0 to 2.6 μM, the sensor exhibited a continuous change in fluorescence color from blue to purple to red. The limit of detection is 1.37 nM with a response time of less than 1 s. Furthermore, this study presents a portable capillary chemo-fluorescent sensor detection kit, which consists of a capillary chemo-fluorescent sensor comprising BCDs/Eu-MOF encapsulated in a self-designed non-fluorescent glass capillary, as well as a color recognizer with a kit produced using 3D printing technology. This fluorescence sensing technology with excellent sensitivity and selectivity provides technical support for the rapid detection of TCs and the reduction of pollutant hazards to sustainable human development.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracyclines antibiotics (TCs), as a class of broad-spectrum antibiotics with the advantages of low cost, low toxicity, and easy oral absorption, including tetracycline (TC), demeclocycline (DMC), doxycycline (DOC), metacycline (MTC), chlortetracycline (CTC), oxytetracycline (OTC), etc., are widely used in fields such as medicine and animal husbandry [1,2]. However, the excessive utilization of TCs has been demonstrated to result in the excretion of substantial quantities of antibiotics into the environment, which not only kills microorganisms in water bodies and damages aquatic ecosystems, but also leads to the accumulation of antibiotics in animal products, such as milk, eggs, and meat, leading to the development of drug resistance in humans and posing a hazard to human health [3–5].

Nowadays, a multitude of conventional techniques are suitable for the detection of TCs in the environment, such as electrochemical sensor [6], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7], flow injection analysis [8] and enzyme-linked immune sorbent assay [9]. Although these detecting methods have high sensitivity and interference resistance, they are subject to certain practical limitations, such as complex sample pretreatment procedures, expensive instrumentation, and long detecting times, which do not allow for rapid on-site detection [10]. Over the past decades, fluorescence sensing technology has received widespread attention for its simplicity, fast response, low cost and high sensitivity [11]. For example, Narasimhappa et al. synthesized blue fluorescent CdS quantum dots by culturing Citrobacter freundii bacteria, and the blue fluorescence of CdS at 400 nm was “turned-off” in the presence of TC, allowing the detection of TC with a limit of detection (LOD) of 23 nM [12]. However, monochromatic fluorescence sensors are susceptible to factors such as photobleaching and sample light scattering, which renders them less suitable for visual detection scenarios. In contrast, ratiometric fluorescence self-calibration is achieved through analyte-induced alterations in the intensity ratios of two or more higher-resolution emission bands, which can produce different fluorescent colors with typical hues, thus reducing the impact of environmental factors and enhances the precision of detection and visual recognition [11,13,14].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOF) represent a novel class of porous materials that are formed by combining metal centers or clusters with organic structural units through ligand bonding, which have a high surface area, adjustable functional sites, high sensitivity, and a robust anti-interference capacity [15–17]. Among them, lanthanide MOF have attracted wide attention due to their advantageous properties, including larger Stokes shift and higher quantum yield [18]. These properties endow them with considerable potential for applications in the domains of fluorescence sensing and environmental detection. Lanthanide MOF can detect a range of substances by fluorescence “turn-on” and “turn-off.” In comparison, the utilization of “turn-on”-type fluorescent sensors as a detection method is a more direct approach, exhibiting reduced susceptibility to interference from other substances, therefore offers significant advantages [19].

In this study, we employed a selective sensing chemo-fluorescent sensor comprising “turn-off”-type blue N-doped carbon dots (BCDs) and “turn-on”-type red europium metal–organic framework (Eu-MOF), which enables visual detection of the full spectrum of TCs with high sensitivity. Initially, the prepared BCDs/Eu-MOF sensor exhibited blue fluorescence at 454 nm under UV light excitation at 350 nm, the blue fluorescence of BCDs was “turned-off” when TCs were present. Conversely, TCs can effectively sensitize Eu3+ in Eu-MOF, resulting in a strong red “turn-on” fluorescence at 616 nm. Consequently, the dual response of ratiometric fluorescent sensors is formed and the visual fluorescent detection can be achieved without the necessity of expensive instrumentation with a LOD of 1.52 nM (DOC), 1.37 nM (DMC), 1.60 nM (MTC), 2.25 nM (CTC), 2.40 nM (TC), 6.49 nM (OTC). Furthermore, the encapsulation of BCDs/Eu-MOF in self-designed non-fluorescent glass capillaries resulted in the development of a portable capillary chemo-fluorescent sensor detection kit for the on-site quantitative and rapid detection of TCs, which enables fluorescence detection of TCs with a LOD of 1.90 nM using TC as a representative within a detection time of less than 1 min. The designed BCDs/Eu-MOF chemo-fluorescent sensing technology addressed the limitations of high cost, dependence on instrumentation, and complexity of operation in real-time quantitative detection, offering a novel approach for areas such as environmental safety detection.

1,2,4-Benzenetricarboxylic acid (BTC), EuCl3·6H2O, anhydrous ethanol, citric acid (CA), polyethyleneimine (PEI), TC, DMC, DOC, MTC, CTC, Lincomycin (LCM), OTC, N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), Roxithromycin (ROX), Piperacillin (PIPC), Clindamycin (CLI), Cefazolin (CEZ), Vancomycin (VAN), Clarithromycin (CAM), Pefloxacin (PEF), Lomefloxacin (LOM), CrCl3·6H2O, Gentamicin (GM), Amoxicillin (AMX), Glufosinate (GS), Cefaclor (CEC), Tetrachlorantraniliprole (TET), FeCl3·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, Cypermethrin (CYP) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co. Ltd. Ultrapure water was obtained using a Millipore water purification system (18.2 MΩ cm−1, Milli-Q). All chemicals were analytically pure and could be used without further purification.

The UV–visible absorption spectra were measured by a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were taken by a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL., Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was measured using an X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical, X'Pert PRO) using Cu Kα radiation with a 2θ range from 5° to 65°. Fluorescence was measured using a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out using a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250 high performance electron spectrometer with Al Kα (1486.6 eV) radiation. Fourier infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured by an IS10 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo-Fisher, USA). Zeta potential test was achieved by the instrument ELSZ-2000 (Japan). The 3D printer was a Makerbot Replicator 2X. The morphology of the Eu-MOF sensor and the element mapping were recorded using a Zeiss Sigma scanning electron microscope (SEM). Brunner-Emmet-Teller surface area and pore size were determined by ASAP 2460 Specific Surface and Pore Size Analyzer. All the fluorescence images were taken under an AGL-9406 portable UV lamp (350 nm) with a Canon EOS600D digital camera. Smart phone (Xiaomi 13, China) was used for fluorescence data collection and processing.

1.0 g of CA was dissolved in 30 mL of ultrapure water, and then 0.50 g of PEI was added at a stirring speed of 800 rpm until the reagent was completely dissolved. The mixed solution was then transferred to an autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and reacted at 200 °C for 6 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the resulting solution was dialyzed through a 1000 Da molecular weight dialysis bag and then the dialyzed solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

0.037 g of EuCl3·6H2O and 0.021 g of BTC were ultrasonically dissolved in 50 ml of DMF. Subsequently, a certain amount of the above-prepared BCDs solution was added and stirred for 30 min. The stirred liquid was transferred to an autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and reacted at 120 °C for 24 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the precipitate was washed three times with anhydrous ethanol and the solid obtained by centrifugation was dissolved in 20 ml of ethanol to obtain BCDs/Eu-MOF, which was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of TCs by BCDs/Eu-MOF chemo-fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Usually, 1.0 ml of BCDs/Eu-MOF sensor was introduced to the cuvette, followed by the addition of a specific amount of TCs with varying concentrations. The fluorescence intensity ratio of I616/I454 was then recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer for the distinct types of TCs. Furthermore, the fluorescence color change was observed under a 350 nm UV lamp and photographed with a digital camera.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Portable capillary chemo-fluorescent sensor detection kit for the detection of TCs
ce:section.ce:para: To satisfy the requirement for the portable and rapid detection of TCs in outdoor and remote areas, a portable capillary chemo-fluorescent sensor detection kit for quantitative detection under UV light was constructed here based on 3D printing and a smartphone color recognition application. The BCDs/Eu-MOF were initially encapsulated using capillaries to form capillary chemo-fluorescent sensors and subsequently stored within the capillary chemo-fluorescent sensor storage rack. Then, the capillary chemo-fluorescent sensor was employed to aspirate a specified quantity of TCs with varying concentrations, incubated in the dark room of the detection box for 30 s. After that, the UV light at 350 nm was turned on and a series of fluorescence images were captured using the smartphone camera. These images were then analyzed to obtain their RGB values. The G/R value was subsequently used as the relevant parameter for the quantitative detection of TCs. Additionally, three images were taken for each concentration to minimize experimental errors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TCs in real complex samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicality of the detection, we chose to use lake water, milk, and honey for the detection of TCs residues. The lake water was collected from a lake near the laboratory, and TC was added to the three water samples at concentrations of 0.02, 0.1, 0.5 and 1 μM after removing impurities by 0.22 μm microporous filtration. Honey and milk were purchased from local supermarkets, and both samples required pre-treatment prior to the experiment. The honey was first diluted with water and then washed by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 3 min, and the supernatant was collected and set aside to remove the polymers contaminating the honey. 15 g of milk was accurately weighed and then 35 ml of anhydrous ethanol was added, thoroughly mixed and allowed to stand for two minutes, the supernatant was collected and set aside. Prior to testing, the supernatants of honey and milk were first filtered using 0.22 μm microporous filtration to remove impurities, to which TC was added to form TC solutions at concentrations of 0.02, 0.1, 0.5 and 1 μM for testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design principle and mechanism study of the BCDs/Eu-MOF chemo-fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 illustrates the synthetic pathway of BCDs/Eu-MOF and its mechanism for visual detection of TCs. N doping can effectively modulate the electronic states and transition channels in the carbon dots system, thereby enhancing the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots [20,21], so that the BCDs show strong blue fluorescence at 454 nm under the excitation of 350 nm UV light, while the fluorescence emission of Eu3+ is very weak due to the blocking of the f-f jump. As illustrated in Fig. S7(b), the FT-IR spectra of BCDs remained unchanged after the addition of TCs, suggesting that BCDs did not undergo a reaction with TCs. However, as illustrated in Fig. S1, the fluorescence excitation spectra of BCDs overlap with the UV–visible absorption peaks of TCs. Consequently, when TCs are present, the competitive absorption based on the internal filtration effect (IFE) results in an evident fluorescence “turn-off” phenomenon in BCDs [22]. The symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrational bands of the carboxylate group of Eu-MOF at 1629–1569 cm−1 and 1492–1339 cm−1 exhibited enhanced absorption subsequent to the incorporation of TCs [23,24]. Concurrently, the UV–Vis absorption curve of Eu-MOF manifested a novel peak at 390 nm following the addition of TCs. These indicate that the β-diketone structure in TCs can forms rare-earth organic complexes (Eu3+-TCs) with Eu3+ [24,25], allowing the energy absorbed by TCs to be transferred from singlet states to triplet states through intersystem crossing, and subsequently transferred to Eu3+ through energy transfer [26,27]. This process effectively sensitizes Eu3+, resulting in a strong red “turn-on” fluorescence at 616 nm [28,29]. Therefore, self-calibration was accomplished through proportional fluorescence, with blue fluorescence being “turned-off” and red fluorescence being “turned-on”, resulting in visual and sensitive detection of TCs.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, the phase purity of the synthesized Eu-MOF was determined by XRD, and the diffraction pattern of the synthesized samples was generally consistent with the data reported in the literature, indicating high purity [30]. To better understand the elemental composition and structure of BCDs and Eu-MOF, we recorded their XPS spectra. As shown in Fig. S2(a), the full-scan spectra indicated that Eu-MOF contained C, Eu, O, and N elemental peaks, while BCDs were mainly composed of C, O, and N elements (Fig. S3(a)). Fig. 1(b) shows that the two peaks at 1165.9 eV and 1135.1 eV in the high-resolution spectrum of Eu 3d can be attributed to the double peaks 3d3/2 and 3d5/2 formed by the spin–orbit coupling of the larger nuclear energy levels of Eu 3d [2]. And the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. S2(b)) can be integrated as three peaks at 284.3 eV, 285.2 eV, and 288.6 eV, corresponding to C-C, O-C/N-C, and O-C=O bonds, respectively [31]. In the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S2(c)), the peaks at 531.2 eV and 532.3 eV correspond to C=O and C-O/C=O bonds, respectively [32]. The presence of N 1 s spectra in the high resolution spectra and bonding with N in the C 1 s spectra may be due to the presence of DMF during the synthesis process. And element mapping further proved the elemental composition of Eu-MOF, as shown in Fig. S4, which demonstrates the presence and distribution of Eu, C, N and O in Eu-MOF. The specific surface area and pore size of Eu-MOF were obtained through nitrogen adsorption–desorption experiments, as illustrated in Fig. 1(c), revealing the presence of mesopores within the Eu-MOF, which exhibited a considerable specific surface area. This resulted in an enrichment effect, enabling the efficient capture of TCs and enhancing their detection sensitivity [33]. Additionally, the abundant number of active sites provided a reduction in reaction time (less than 1 s, Video S1 and Fig. S5) [34], conferring rapid detection capability to BCDs/Eu-MOF for TCs.

The synthesized Eu-MOF can be observed to exhibit a spherical micrometer flower structure as seen in Fig. 1(d) via SEM. Additionally, the sheet structure of the nanoflower is also evident in Fig. 1e, with a diameter of approximately 25 μm. It can also be seen from the TEM iamge (Fig. 1(f)) that the diameter of the BCDs is around 1.5 nm. As illustrated in Fig. S6, HRTEM images reveal that Eu-MOFs and BCDs exhibit crystal plane spacings of 0.22 nm and 0.12 nm, respectively, demonstrating their good crystallinity. To investigate the alterations in Eu-MOF prior to and following the addition of TCs, we examined the UV–vis absorption spectra of Eu-MOF, Eu-MOF + TCs and TCs (Fig. 1g). And the Eu-MOF + TCs spectra exhibited the emergence of a novel absorption peak, the new absorption peak was observed at approximately 390 nm, in comparison to the absorption peak of Eu-MOF. Furthermore, the position of the new absorption peak was found to be redshifted by 33 nm in relation to the position of the absorption peak of TCs at that location. This phenomenon can be attributed to the formation of rare earth-organic complexes generated by Eu-MOF with TCs, which shifts the absorption frequency to a lower position [24,25]. In addition, the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrational bands of the carboxylate group of Eu-MOF and BCDs/Eu-MOF at 1629–1569 cm−1 and 1492–1339 cm−1 exhibited enhanced absorption subsequent to the incorporation of TCs (Fig. 1(h) and Fig. S7(a)), further suggesting that Eu-MOF generated rare earth-organic complexes with TCs [23,24]. In contrast, BCDs do not undergo such a reaction, it can be seen from the FT-IR spectra of BCDs and BCDs + TCs (Fig. S7(b)) that there is no notable alteration in the spectra prior to and following the addition of TCs, suggesting that there was no mutual reaction between TCs and BCDs. As illustrated in Fig. S8, a positive correlation exists between the absolute value of Zeta potential and system stability [35], the absolute values of the Zeta potentials of BCDs, Eu-MOF, and BCDs/Eu-MOF increase after the addition of TCs at pH = 7, substantiating the occurrence of energy transfer between these components. These phenomena indicate that TCs interact with Eu-MOF to “turn-on” the red fluorescence, whereas BCDs “turn-off” the blue fluorescence through IFE without reaction. As illustrated in Fig. 1(i), the optimal excitation conditions for BCDs/Eu-MOF can be determined by adding a specific amount of TCs to the BCDs/Eu-MOF sensors and utilizing different wavelengths of UV excitation. The results demonstrate that the optimal excitation wavelength for BCDs/Eu-MOF is 350 nm, which produces the most effective “turn-on” effect of Eu-MOF. At this time, BCDs also have good fluorescence effect, so 350 nm was adopted as the excitation wavelength of BCDs/Eu-MOF fluorescent sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive ratiometric fluorescence detection of TCs with BCDs/Eu-MOF chemo-fluorescent sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of TCs can be achieved by means of fluorescent sensors through the changes in fluorescence intensity and fluorescence color. Consequently, following the introduction of TCs into BCDs/Eu-MOF, the blue fluorescence generated by BCDs is effectively ''turned-off'' by TCs based on IFE, thereby showing the red fluorescence of the Eu-MOF sensitized by TCs, thus enabling the detection of TCs through this color shift. This transition from blue to purple to red occurred in a gradual and continuous manner. In comparison to monochromatic fluorescence changes, ratiometric fluorescence is less susceptible to environmental interference, thereby enabling more accurate visualization of the detection, and the visualization effect of the fluorescent sensor is largely determined by the fluorescence intensity ratio I616/I454. Accordingly, the incorporation of varying quantities of BCDs into the synthesis of BCDs/Eu-MOF will result in disparate fluorescence ratios, consequently leading to distinct visualization outcomes. As illustrated in Fig. S9, the most effective and discernible visualization of fluorescence alterations was attained when the fluorescence ratio was 1.8, in comparison to other ratios. This effect was observed across a wider concentration detection range, from bright blue to bright red, allowing for the addition to be identified as the optimal condition for visual detection. In consideration of the potential impact of temperature, pH, and other conditions on the fluorescent probe's efficacy during the detection process, a series of conditions were examined. As illustrated in Fig. S10, within the temperature range of 10 ∼ 50 °C, the fluorescence intensity of BCDs/Eu-MOF with a specific quantity of TCs incorporated initially increased and then decreased with rising temperature. In contrast, its fluorescence intensity ratio remained relatively constant, indicating that the sensor exhibited high stability. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity ratio remained relatively stable across different pH levels (Fig. S11). As illustrated in Fig. S5, Fig. S12 and Video S1, the incorporation of TCs into the BCDs/Eu-MOF sensors enables a rapid reaction within 1 s and sustained stability for up to 720 h. Consequently, these indicate that the designed fluorescent probe exhibits excellent temperature stability and acid and alkali resistance, and can be detected over a wide temperature and pH range while maintaining good stability.

Fig. S13 and Fig. S14 demonstrate the monochromatic fluorescence spectra of BCDs and Eu-MOF for the detection of TCs. Under UV light excitation at a wavelength of 350 nm, the blue fluorescence intensity of BCDs at 454 nm experiences a gradual ''turn-off'' in response to the increasing concentration of TCs. Conversely, the red fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOF displays a corresponding gradual enhancement in response to the increasing concentration of TCs. Nonetheless, the shift in monochromatic fluorescence is susceptible to environmental influences. Concurrently, the human eye is not sensitive to the perception of changes in monochromatic fluorescence. Fig. 2 illustrates the fluorescence spectra of the BCDs/Eu-MOF chemo-fluorescent sensor for the detection of TCs under 350 nm UV light excitation, as well as its fluorescence pictures. In the absence of TCs, the BCDs/Eu-MOF exhibits a strong blue fluorescence at 454 nm. As the concentration of TCs increased from 0 to 2.6 μM, the blue fluorescence at 454 nm was gradually “turned-off”, while the fluorescence intensity of the red fluorescence at 616 nm demonstrated a notable enhancement. Additionally, the fluorescence photographs illustrated a discernible shift in fluorescence color from blue to purple to red as the concentration of TCs increased, allowing for visual detection. The ratiometric fluorescent sensor, formed by this color change from blue to red, can effectively eliminate the interference caused by the external environment and solve the problem of visual detection of insignificant changes in monochromatic fluorescence, etc., thus producing more accurate detection results.

Furthermore, the I616/I454 fluorescence intensity ratio of BCDs/Eu-MOF demonstrated a favorable dual-stage linear correlation with the concentration of TCs (Fig. 3 ). The R2 of the high concentration fraction were 0.9885 (DOC), 0.9702 (DMC), 0.9977 (MTC), 0.9973 (CTC), 0.9936 (TC) and 0.9926 (OTC), respectively. Whereas, the R2 of the low concentration fraction were 0.9529 (DOC), 0.9600 (DMC), 0.9595 (MTC), 0.9630 (CTC), 0.9530 (TC) and 0.9530 (OTC). The LOD were 1.52 nM (DOC), 1.37 nM (DMC), 1.60 nM (MTC), 2.25 nM (CTC), 2.40 nM (TC), 6.49 nM (OTC), respectively. The LOD was estimated based on LOD = 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal (n = 9), and k is the slope of the calibration plot [36], which shows a lower LOD with better linearity. In comparison to the detection limits and response times reported in previous studies (Table S1), the proposed sensor demonstrates superior performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and immunity of ratiometric BCDs/Eu-MOF fluorescent chemo-fluorescent sensors
ce:section.ce:para: In the detection of real samples, other kinds of interfering substances are often present in the samples to be detected. Therefore, we conducted a series of tests to assess the fluorescence detection effect of BCDs/Eu-MOF in the presence of a range of interfering substances, including representative pesticides (CYP, GS, TET), heavy metals (Cr3+, Fe3+, Cu2+), and antibiotics (LCM, PIPC, CLI, ROX, CEZ, VAN, CAM, PEF, LOM, GM, AMX, CEC). As illustrated in Fig. 4 (a), the introduction of these interferences did not result in any discernible alteration in fluorescence intensity. The red fluorescence of Eu-MOF could not be “turned-on”, while the blue fluorescence intensity of BCDs exhibited a slight decrease, which led to a notable alteration in fluorescence was observed exclusively when TCs were introduced. The fluorescent photographs in Fig. 4(c) also demonstrated the specific detection ability of TCs, which could be observed by the visual detection. This indicated that BCDs/Eu-MOF exhibited excellent selectivity. Furthermore, the addition of interfering substances did not affect the detection of TCs by the BCDs/Eu-MOF sensing system. As illustrated in the anti-interference experiment depicted in Fig. 4(b), the specific detection capability of BCDs/Eu-MOF remains unaffected when interferents coexist with TCs. Therefore, it can be concluded that the chemo-fluorescent sensor exhibits excellent selectivity and anti-interference capability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of a portable capillary chemo-fluorescent sensor detection kit
ce:section.ce:para: To broaden the application scenarios of BCDs/Eu-MOF sensors and simplify the operational procedures involved in fluorescent sensor detection, we have developed a portable capillary chemo-fluorescent sensor detection kit for the rapid detection of TCs based on 3D printing technology and a smartphone-based color recognizer. As illustrated in Fig. 5 (a), the portable capillary chemo-fluorescent sensor detection kit is comprised primarily of the detection kit body, capillary chemo-fluorescent sensors consisting of self-designed non-fluorescent glass capillaries encapsulating BCDs/Eu-MOF, a capillary storage rack, a cell phone holder, a smartphone, and a compact UV lamp. Fig. S15 provides a comprehensive illustration of the diverse range of accessories associated with the detection cartridge, and specific portable capillary chemo-fluorescent sensor detection kit operation can be seen in the Video S2. Initially, a specific quantity of BCDs/Eu-MOF is encapsulated within a self-designed non-fluorescent glass capillary (liquid level 1) to form a capillary chemo-fluorescent sensor and stored in the capillary chemo-fluorescent sensors storage rack. Upon utilization, a designated volume of the liquid to be detected is aspirated via the capillary chemo-fluorescent sensor (reaching liquid level 2) and subsequently placed into the sample slot, with the lid of the detection box secured to provide a dark environment, following a 30 s incubation period, the UV lamp was turned on. As shown in Fig. 5(b), a color photograph of the fluorescence was then rapidly captured and converted to RGB values by the color recognition application on the smartphone. The concentration of TCs was subsequently evaluated by calculating the ratio of the red to the blue channel and the result was then displayed on the smartphone. As illustrated in Fig. 5(c), using TC as a representative, the ratios (R/G) of the red channel to the blue channel demonstrate a strong dual-stage linear correlation with the concentration of TC, with R2 of 0.9917 (high concentration) and 0.9660 (low concentration), and a LOD of 1.90 nM. The constructed simple and portable real-time detection platform could sensitively detect TCs within one minute of operation, and the ratiometric fluorescence exhibited a good linear relationship with TC at low (0.0019–1 μM) and high (1–2.6 μM) concentrations, providing a promising strategy for public safety and food safety detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TCs in real complex samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further assess the feasibility of BCDs/Eu-MOF in real complex sample detection, we employed the constructed chemo-fluorescent sensor for the identification of TCs in lake water, milk, and honey. The samples were initially subjected to a pre-treatment phase, after which TC was introduced as a representative to form the real complex sample solutions, which were prepared at concentrations of 0.02, 0.1, 0.5 and 1 μM for subsequent analysis. The recoveries ranged from 97.20 % to 108.50 %, and the relative standard deviations were from 0.99 % to 3.85 %, as can be seen in Table 1 . These results show that the chemo-fluorescent sensor we constructed can be used in real complex samples. In addition, we employed HPLC to validate our results for the detection of TCs in complex samples. As illustrated in Table S2, the outcomes obtained with BCDs/Eu-MOF are basically the same as those achieved via HPLC, thereby substantiating the feasibility and accuracy of the BCDs/Eu-MOF in the detection of complex samples.

In summary, we have developed a novel BCDs/Eu-MOF chemo-fluorescence sensor for the detection of the full spectrum of TCs, where TCs can “turn-off” the blue fluorescence of BCDs by IFE, while sensitizing the Eu3+ of Eu-MOF to transfer the absorbed energy to Eu3+ which results in intense red fluorescence. This allows the fluorescence of the sensor to achieve a visual continuous color change from blue to purple to red, with a low LOD (from 1.37 to 6.49 nM) and a fast response speed (less than 1 s). In addition, the detection technique exhibits an effective detection capability of TCs in real complex samples, thereby making it suitable for the detection of TCs in intricate samples. Furthermore, the sensor can be encapsulated in a self-designed non-fluorescent glass capillary and combined with a smartphone color recognition application to form a portable capillary chemo-fluorescent sensor detection kit, enabling the fast and sensitive detection of TCs residues with a LOD as low as 1.90 nM using TC as a representative. The developed selective sensing chemo-fluorescent sensor is characterized by rapidity, simplicity, high sensitivity and ease of operation, thereby providing a reliable method for quantitative detection of TCs in the environment and technical support for the development of selective sensing chemo-fluorescent sensors.

Yaowei Zhu: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ruijie Tang: Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis. Jun Zhao: Validation, Project administration, Formal analysis. Changlong Jiang: Software, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Lingfei Li: Writing – original draft, Validation, Resources, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Bianhua Liu: Software, Methodology, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by National Key Research and Development Program (2021YFD2000202), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos, 22274158), Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Provincial (2208085 MB30), Open project funding for the Key Laboratory of Agricultural Sensors of the Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, and Open project funding for the Key Laboratory of Smart Agricultural Technology and Equipment of Anhui Province. (KLAS2022KF001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this paper is available. Supporting Information Available: Includes Excitation and fluorescence spectra of BCDs and UV–vis absorption spectra of TCs, XPS data of Eu-MOF and BCDs, Element mapping analysis of Eu-MOF, TEM and HRTEM images of Eu-MOF and BCDs, FT-IR spectra of BCDs/Eu-MOF, BCDs/Eu-MOF + TCs, BCDs and BCDs + TCs, Characterization of pH, temperature, optimal fluorescence ratio, stability of fluorescence sensing systems, Zeta potential data of TCs, BCDs, BCDs + TCs, Eu-MOF, Eu-MOF + TCs, BCDs/Eu-MOF, BCDs/Eu-MOF + TCs, Fluorescence spectra of BCDs and Eu-MOF when different amounts of TCs were added, Portable capillary chemo-fluorescent sensor detection kit composition, Literature comparison of fluorescent sensors and Determination of TCs spiked in real complex samples by HPLC.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2025.160281.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
